query: Latest in web performance optimization, CDN technologies, and caching strategies
raw_intelligence:
The short answer: focus on (1) reducing client-side work (remove/defert/partition JS, inline-critical CSS, modern image formats, lazy-loading), (2) shifting delivery to edge/CDN and using advanced CDN features (edge compute, origin shielding, tiered cache, HTTP/3+QUIC, image/video optimization), and (3) a layered caching strategy (browser caching + service worker + CDN + origin caching with cache-control, surrogate-keys, and smart invalidation) combined with instrumentation for Core Web Vitals and AI-mode/LLM visibility. These trends are now central to web performance optimization and SEO/AI visibility efforts.[1][5][3]

Key items and actionable details

1) Client-side reductions and Core Web Vitals
- Prioritize reducing JavaScript execution time: remove unused JS, split large bundles, and use code-splitting/HTTP/2/HTTP/3-friendly module loading to lower INP and LCP impacts.[1][5]  
- Inline critical CSS for above-the-fold rendering and purge unused CSS (PurgeCSS, coverage tools) to improve LCP and render times.[1]  
- Use modern image formats (AVIF, WebP) and responsive srcset; convert hero images to AVIF and serve fallbacks for compatibility.[1][2]  
- Lazy-load offscreen images and nonessential iframes, and defer noncritical scripts to reduce blocking and TTFB-to-render latency.[1][2]

2) CDN technologies and edge compute (what’s new and important)
- Edge compute on CDNs (serverless functions at the edge) is mainstream: use it for personalization, A/B tests, authentication, and small transforms to avoid round trips to origin.[4][3]  
- Protocol upgrades: deploy HTTP/3 (QUIC) to reduce connection setup and improve performance on lossy networks; many CDNs now support HTTP/3 by default.[2][4]  
- Tiered caching / origin shielding: CDNs offer multi-tier caches and origin shielding to reduce origin load and cache-miss latency — configure tiered caching when you have global traffic patterns.[2]  
- Built-in media optimization: use CDN image/video optimization (auto format conversion, responsive resizing, AVIF/WebP delivery, AV1 for video when supported) to shrink payloads on the fly.[1][2]  
- Edge caching of computed HTML (ISR-style / edge-side rendering): cache pre-rendered HTML at the edge with short revalidation windows to serve dynamic pages quickly while keeping freshness.[3][4]

3) Caching strategies (practical layered approach)
- Browser caching: set Cache-Control (max-age, immutable for hashed assets) and use long cache lifetimes for fingerprinted assets; use short max-age or no-cache for HTML with validation headers.[1][2]  
- CDN caching: cache static assets aggressively; use surrogate-control or CDN-specific TTLs for different asset classes and implement cache key normalization (ignore irrelevant query strings).[2]  
- Origin and conditional caching: support ETags and Last-Modified for conditional GETs; combine with CDN origin shielding and rate limiting to protect origin.[2][3]  
- Cache invalidation and surrogate-keys: implement tag-based invalidation (surrogate-keys) for group invalidation instead of purging individual URLs.[2]  
- Service workers: use a service worker for offline-first, fine-grained caching and faster repeat navigations (stale-while-revalidate patterns), but keep the service worker logic small and secure.[1][2]  
- Cache hierarchy example: immutable hashed static assets → long CDN TTL → long browser TTL; dynamic HTML → short CDN TTL + stale-while-revalidate → browser no-cache with validation.[2][1]

4) Instrumentation, observability, and AI/SEO implications
- Monitor Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and field telemetry (RUM) before relying on lab metrics; tie lab changes to real-user metrics to avoid regressions.[5][3]  
- AI-mode / LLM visibility: performance still matters for AI citations — better INP/LCP correlates with higher chance of being surfaced in AI/LLM outputs, alongside authority and content quality.[5]  
- Use synthetic testing (Lighthouse, WebPageTest) plus RUM (CrUX, analytics) and continuous measurement in CI to catch regressions.[1][5]

5) Security, sustainability, and developer workflow
- Security headers, HTTPS, and signed exchanges (where applicable) remain necessary for performance + SEO.[3][6]  
- Sustainable web design (leaner code, image optimization, green hosting) reduces carbon and often improves speed; evaluate hosting/CDN providers for sustainability commitments when relevant.[4]  
- Integrate performance checks into CI/CD and use automated budgets (bundle size, TTFB) and PR gating to prevent regressions.[3]

Practical checklist (prioritized)
- Audit: run RUM + Lighthouse + WebPageTest to find major JS/CSS/image bottlenecks.[1][5]  
- Asset hygiene: remove unused JS/CSS, split bundles, adopt modern image formats and responsive sizes.[1][2]  
- Cache plan: fingerprint assets, set long Cache-Control for hashed files, implement surrogate-keys on CDN, enable tiered caching and origin shielding.[2]  
- Edge: enable HTTP/3, enable CDN image transforms, and move small server logic to edge functions for personalization/A/B testing.[2][4]  
- Service worker: implement stale-while-revalidate and offline caching for repeat visits but keep logic minimal.[2][1]  
- Observe: deploy RUM dashboards for Core Web Vitals, tie to business KPIs (bounce, conversion) and automate alerts for regressions.[5][3]

When to prioritize which technique
- High JS execution times / poor INP: focus on code-splitting, removing third-party scripts, and reducing main-thread work.[1][5]  
- Large images / heavy media: use CDN image optimization + AVIF/WebP + responsive srcset.[1][2]  
- Global audience / origin cost: enable CDN tiered caching, origin shielding, and aggressive CDN caching.[2][4]  
- Frequent dynamic updates: use edge caching with short revalidation and surrogate-key invalidation for efficient freshness.[3][2]

Limitations and trade-offs
- Service workers and aggressive edge HTML caching add complexity for consistency and invalidation; implement careful versioning and monitoring.[2][3]  
- Edge compute can increase operational surface area and cost; reserve it for latency-sensitive logic that benefits end users.[4]  
- AVIF/AV1 offer big savings but require fallbacks; measure client support and provide polyfills or alternative formats where needed.[1]

If you want, I can:
- Run a concise diagnostic checklist you can apply to a specific site (commands, what to look for in Lighthouse/RUM).  
- Propose CDN configuration snippets (Cache-Control, surrogate-keys, edge function pseudocode) for your stack (Vercel/Cloudflare/Akamai/Fastly).  
- Identify the 3–5 highest-impact quick wins for a given URL if you share one.
